---
title: "Tyler Rongxuan Chen"
subtitle: "Ph.D. Candidate in Political Science & Scientific Computing, University of Michigan"
image: assets/profile.jpg
about:
  template: solana
  image-shape: rounded
  image-width: 16em
  links:
    - icon: envelope
      text: Email
      href: "mailto:tylercr@umich.edu"
    - icon: file-earmark-pdf
      text: CV
      href: assets/Tyler_Rongxuan_Chen_CV.pdf
    # TODO: uncomment and fill in once you have a Google Scholar profile URL
    # - icon: mortarboard-fill
    #   text: Google Scholar
    #   href: "https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=YOUR-ID"
    - icon: building
      text: Department Profile
      href: "https://prod.lsa.umich.edu/polisci/people/graduate-students/tyler-chen.html"
---

::: {.lead}
Hi, I'm Tyler! Welcome to my website.
:::

Tyler Rongxuan Chen is a Ph.D. candidate in Political Science and Scientific Computing at the
University of Michigan, where he is also completing an M.A. in Statistics. Trained as a social
demographer and a fellow of Michigan's Population Studies Center, he studies democracy through its
parties and party systems: when and how they realign or come apart as the population groups beneath
them move, grow, shrink, and re-cluster across space and time.

His dissertation traces these scale effects across the United States, Malaysia, Taiwan, and Japan.
Methodology is the other half of his work: he is a political methodologist who builds the tools
needed to recover who votes for whom when surveys fail. His flagship contribution — the Bayesian
Approximate Computation with Hierarchical framework (BACH) — recovers subgroup voting behavior from
aggregate election returns where conventional estimators break down, and around it sits a broader
toolkit: a higher-order method-of-moments estimator for elections without reliable surveys, spatial
econometrics for electorates whose district boundaries move, and locally run large-language-model
pipelines for coding archival text.

His research appears in the *Journal of East Asian Studies*, and he works with primary sources in
Chinese, Malay, and Indonesian. He has taught across Michigan's quantitative methods sequence —
from the graduate statistics core to undergraduate data science — as well as social demography
(*World Population Dynamics*), and has been a teaching assistant at the Inter-university Consortium
for Political and Social Research (ICPSR) Summer Program.

He is on the 2026–27 academic job market.

Outside the academy, he volunteers as a cat foster with the
[Humane Society of Huron Valley](https://www.hshv.org/).
